text: The surgeon prepares to use four laparoscopic ports for the procedure, positioning them strategically for optimal access to the surgical site.
The surgeon demonstrates the irreducibility of the left inguinal scrotal hernia, which remains incarcerated despite attempts at manual palpation.
The surgeon begins the procedure by creating a peritoneal flap, starting laterally and progressing medially, while maintaining dissection within the preperitoneal plane.
The surgeon creates a large peritoneal flap, providing clear exposure of the surgical field and ensuring visibility of all corners without tearing the flap.
The surgeon uses a Cartier instrument to retract the peritoneal flap, allowing both hands to work in coordination during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a Cartier retractor to hold the peritoneal flap, freeing the left hand to assist the right hand in retracting tissue, ensuring a safe and dynamic dissection process.
The surgeon addresses the space of Retzius, carefully dissecting as far as possible without entering the deeper retropubic space, ensuring a manageable working area for the hernia repair.
The surgeon carefully dissects downwards until Cooper's ligament is visible, ensuring to stay close to the peritoneum laterally.
The surgeon identifies a clear anatomical plane, noting its position relative to the testicular vessels, which are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon opens a plane between the testicular vessels and the hernia contents, carefully separating these structures to facilitate further dissection.
The surgeon carefully uses cautery on the outer side of the tissue, avoiding the colon side, while pulling away to ensure safe dissection.
The surgeon ensures hemostasis in the surgical field, emphasizing the importance of maintaining clear visibility of the testicular vessels during the dissection of a difficult hernia.
The surgeon pauses upon encountering tissue that appears to belong to the colon and decides to investigate an alternative plane, ensuring careful dissection to avoid damaging the colon.

The surgeon identifies the blue tinge of the testicular vein, confirming the presence of a plane between the testicular vessels and the colon. The dissection continues to separate these retroperitoneal structures.

The surgeon carefully dissects between the testicular vessels and the colon, establishing a clear plane between these structures. This maneuver is crucial in cases with large, incarcerated hernia contents to facilitate tracing the plane into the inguinal canal or distal part of the hernia.
The surgeon begins reducing the hernia sac, focusing on dividing the transverse salus sling that wraps around the anterior and medial portion of the sac.
The surgeon carefully dissects and removes attachments around the hernia sac, increasing its mobility and facilitating its gradual reduction from the inguinal canal.
The surgeon dissects around the hernia sac between the nine and three o'clock positions, using cautery safely in this area. The inferior epigastric vessels are kept in view to avoid damage.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the hernia contents, maintaining a plane outside of the testicular vessels. This plane is visually identifiable as the easier path for dissection, allowing for subsequent reduction of hernia contents before locating the plane between the testicular vessels and the hernia sac.
The surgeon attempts to reduce the hernia by applying pressure on the scrotum, assisted by a bedside technician, after dissecting part of the hernia sac.
The surgeon attempts to reduce the hernia contents by starting a flap and dissecting it, but the contents remain non-reducible.

The surgeon continues to dissect around the hernia sac, which begins to slide out of the inguinal canal without the need to divide any tissue. The flimsy attachments to the hernia sac are carefully taken down, allowing the hernia contents to slowly reduce and eventually fully reduce.
The surgeon uses the fourth arm of the robotic system to provide retraction, splaying out the tissue to differentiate between the hernia sac, connective tissue, and bowel.

The surgeon searches for thin, transparent tissue while considering the location of the testicular vessels and vas deferens. The dissection progresses posteriorly, focusing on maintaining a plane between the testicular vessels and the colon.
The surgeon observes the surgical field where the testicular vessels are wrapping almost anteriorly, making it difficult to distinguish them from the colon and hernia contents.
The surgeon carefully separates the testicular vessels from the colon, ensuring clear identification and avoiding injury during the dissection.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm to provide counter traction during dissection, allowing most of the testicular vessels to be separated from surrounding tissues. Some dissection remains at the tip, which will be addressed later when transitioning from an anterior to posterior approach.

The surgeon identifies the tip of the sac, using retraction to splay out the tissue and locate the transparent structures. The vas deferens is noted as a posterior structure, and cautery is safely applied between the nine and three o'clock positions.

The surgeon focuses on achieving hemostasis while dissecting around a large hernia. They work to expose the medial preperitoneal space (MPO) and parietalize the spermatic cord, ensuring a clear posterior landing zone for mesh placement.

The surgeon carefully removes hernia contents from the cord structures and the psoas muscle, revealing a small hematoma likely caused by earlier bleeding from the testicular vessels. The manipulation of the cord structures is performed without grasping the testicular vessels.

The surgeon uses a grasper to gently push tissue away, avoiding direct contact with nerves. The dissection of the space of Retzius is completed, crossing the midline to ensure the mesh will lay properly. The surgeon then dissects two centimeters deep into the retropubic space.
The surgeon continues to parietalize the cord structures to ensure the mesh can be properly positioned between the colon, testicular vessels, and psoas muscle.

The surgeon prepares to fit an extra-large 3D Max mid mesh into the dissected space, ensuring it is appropriately sized for the large inguinal hernia, with dimensions approximately twelve centimeters.

The surgeon continues dissection in the pelvic region, ensuring the mesh will tuck nicely into place. The dissection occurs in a slightly different plane, with the genitofemoral and lateral femoral cutaneous nerves visible and well protected in the posterior region.
The surgeon completes the dissection medially to ensure the transition from the retropubic space to the abagrel space is clear of tissue, preparing the area for mesh placement.

The surgeon displays the Bard 3D Max-Mid XL mesh, highlighting its macro porosity that allows visibility through the mesh for precise placement. The mesh's three-dimensional contour is shown fitting snugly against the pelvic anatomy.

The surgeon positions the mesh, aligning the vertical blue line along the vas deferens or iliac vein. The mesh is fixated at Cooper's ligament, laterally, and an additional fixation point is added anterior medially.
The surgeon fixates the mesh at Cooper's ligament to prevent the lower edge from lifting, and applies lateral fixation to keep the posterior edge securely in place, avoiding clamshelling.
The surgeon places the final fixation stitch on the mesh, using the fourth arm of the surgical instrument to efficiently align and close the flap over the mesh.
The surgeon uses a six-inch 3-0 V-Loc suture to close a large flap, demonstrating the efficiency of working with a shorter suture length.

Near the end of the flap closure, the surgeon decreases the pneumoperitoneum to six millimeters mercury and uses the fourth arm to press the flap against the colon, evacuating CO₂ from the preperitoneal space. After closure, the pneumoperitoneum is increased to fifteen, causing the peritoneum to visibly appose against the mesh, allowing inspection of the posterior edge for clamshelling.
The surgeon demonstrates that the lower edge of the mesh remains securely positioned against the underlying tissue, preventing any lifting or clamshell effect.
